articles.title: About this site
articles.description: Some additional details about the website
articles.contents:
Home


  Profile


  my work


  About

      
  






About this site





About This Website
This website was created for my GV300 module (Advanced Quantitative
Politics) at the University of Essex.
It serves as a portfolio where I present examples of my data analysis
and research work.
On this site, you can see how I:
Analyse data using R
Create clear and easy-to-understand visualisations
Explain statistical results in simple language
Present academic work in a structured and organised way


How This Website Was Built
This website was built using:
R Markdown to combine writing and code in one
document
Distill and Postcards themes to create a clean and
professional layout
Git and GitHub to manage and track changes
GitHub Pages to publish the website online
The goal was to build a simple and professional academic portfolio
that clearly demonstrates my skills in quantitative analysis and data
communication.

articles.description: BSc Economics and Politics | University of Essex
articles.contents:
Home


  Profile


  my work


  About

      
  






Muhammad Ahmad Hassan





About Me
I am a final-year BSc (Hons) Economics and Politics student at the
University of Essex. My academic interests focus on understanding how
markets function and how political institutions shape economic
outcomes.
Beyond my studies, I enjoy reading widely to gain new perspectives on
economic and political issues. I also play golf in my free time.

articles.title: GV300 Showcase
articles.contents:
Introduction
This page presents selected work from GV300 (Advanced Quantitative Politics).
It demonstrates my ability to analyse data in R and communicate results clearly.
Example Analysis
Here I will include:
- Data visualisations
- Statistical models
- Interpretation of results
visualization
example from assignment 2
In this example, I studied how the number of voters differs between constituencies in the United Kingdom.
The main question I was asking was:
Are all votes equal across constituencies?
In a fair system, each Member of Parliament (MP) should represent a similar number of voters.
However, if some constituencies are much larger than others, then some MPs represent many more people. This could mean that votes do not have equal weight everywhere in the country.
To investigate this,what I did was to:
Download official UK election data
Clean and organise the data using R
Calculate the number of valid votes in each constituency
Use a boxplot to compare differences between countries
The aim of this analysis was to show that I can work with real data, create clear visualisations, and explain statistical results in a simple and understandable way.
Data and Setup


library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(ggplot2)

# Load UK election data directly from official source
uk_csv <- "https://electionresults.parliament.uk/general-elections/6/candidacies.csv"
uk_raw <- readr::read_csv(uk_csv, show_col_types = FALSE)

# Clean column names
names(uk_raw) <- names(uk_raw) %>%
  tolower() %>%
  str_replace_all("\\s+", "_")

# Data cleaning
uk <- uk_raw %>%
  filter(!`election_is_by-election`) %>%
  select(
    constituency = constituency_name,
    country      = country_name,
    valid_votes  = candidate_vote_count,
    electorate   = electorate
  ) %>%
  group_by(country, constituency) %>%
  summarise(
    valid_votes = sum(valid_votes, na.rm = TRUE),
    electorate  = max(electorate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(turnout_registered = 100 * valid_votes / electorate) %>%
  filter(is.finite(turnout_registered), electorate > 0)

ggplot(uk, aes(x = country, y = valid_votes, fill = country)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Constituency Size by Country",
    subtitle = "Boxplots show medians and variation clearly",
    x = "Country",
    y = "Valid Votes"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    legend.position = "none"
  )



#Visualising Constituency Size Differences
I compared variation across countries by using a boxplot.
The Boxplot showed the median, spread, and outlier for each country.
Interpretation
The boxplot shows that constituency sizes are not the same across the UK.
England has a wide range of constituency sizes. Some are much bigger than others.
Scotland has a few very small constituencies, which appear as dots outside the main box (these are called outliers).
Wales seems to have slightly smaller typical constituency sizes compared to England.
Northern Ireland’s constituencies are more similar to each other, meaning there is less variation.
Overall, the graph shows that the number of voters per constituency is not equal across the UK.
This means that some Members of Parliament (MPs) represent many more voters than others.
In larger constituencies, each vote may carry slightly less influence compared to smaller constituencies.
This raises important questions about fairness and equal representation in elections.
Conclusion
This analysis shows that I can:
Clean and organise real election data
Use R to summarise and group data correctly
Create clear and easy-to-read visualisations
Explain statistical results in simple and understandable language
The code is fully reproducible, meaning anyone can run it and get the same results.
The dataset is downloaded directly from the official UK Parliament source.
